proposals.title: Social and Environmental Priming of Neural Plasticity in the Honey Bee Brain: Dissociating the Effects of Foraging Experience
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in the Robinson lab established that foraging experience induces profound structural and molecular changes in the honey bee brain, a key model for experience-dependent neural plasticity. However, it remains unknown whether these changes are exclusively driven by the sensory-motor demands of foraging, or if they can be primed or induced by social context and hive environment alone. Resolving this is critical for understanding the fundamental rules of social modulation of brain states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that social interactions and hive-derived olfactory/chemical cues, in the absence of actual foraging flight, are sufficient to induce gene expression and neuroanatomical changes in brain regions associated with learning and memory (e.g., mushroom bodies).
proposals.method: We will use a cross-fostering design with uniquely barcoded bees (as in Robinson's prior AI-tracking work) to create experimental cohorts. Newly eclosed bees will be introduced into either a normal hive or a hive confined to a flight cage, preventing foraging but allowing full social interaction. Control groups will forage normally. After 7-10 days, brains will be processed for high-throughput sequencing (RNA-seq) to quantify differential gene expression in mushroom body microdissections, and for confocal microscopy analysis of Kenyon cell dendritic arborization. Gene co-expression networks will be constructed using Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis (WGCNA) to identify modules correlated with social vs. foraging experience.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the sufficiency of social context for driving brain plasticity, moving beyond correlative studies. It will identify a core set of socially-responsive genes and neural changes, providing a mechanistic link between social life and brain architecture. The findings will refine models of behavioral maturation in complex societies.
proposals.novelty: While foraging-induced plasticity is well-documented, systematically isolating social-hive effects from foraging is novel. The integration of individual barcoding for precise life-history tracking with neural molecular profiling allows an unprecedented dissection of experience components.

proposals.title: Linking Foraging Experience to Transcriptomic and Proteomic Signatures of Structural Plasticity in the Honey Bee Mushroom Body
proposals.motivation: The honey bee mushroom body is a premier model for experience-dependent neural plasticity, known to undergo structural changes in response to foraging. However, the specific molecular pathways that translate foraging experience into these lasting anatomical changes remain poorly characterized. Identifying these pathways is crucial for understanding how social behavior shapes brain architecture and cognitive function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition to foraging induces a specific, time-regulated program of gene expression and protein synthesis in the mushroom body Kenyon cells, and that this molecular program is directly responsible for driving the observed synaptic growth and dendritic remodeling.
proposals.method: We will use automated hive monitoring with RFID tags to precisely stage foraging onset in individual, barcoded bees. Mushroom body tissue will be collected at critical time points (pre-foraging, early foraging, experienced foraging) for paired transcriptomic (RNA-seq) and proteomic (LC-MS/MS) profiling. Differential expression analysis will identify candidate plasticity genes, which will be functionally validated via RNAi knockdown in the bee brain followed by high-resolution confocal microscopy to quantify structural changes in the mushroom body neuropil.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between specific molecular effectors and foraging-induced neural restructuring. It will provide a comprehensive molecular atlas of experience-dependent plasticity in a key social insect brain region, offering insights into the evolution of behavioral specialization.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond correlative observations by integrating precise behavioral staging from automated monitoring with multi-omics profiling and functional genetic validation specifically within the mushroom body, a targeted approach not previously combined in this model.

proposals.title: Mapping Adult Neurogenesis in the Honey Bee Brain: A Cross-Species Molecular and Computational Analysis
proposals.motivation: Adult neurogenesis is a critical substrate for behavioral plasticity in vertebrates, but its extent and functional role in insects, particularly in a model of complex social behavior like the honey bee, remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 work by the Robinson lab has established links between bee social behavior, brain gene expression, and neural circuits, yet a direct, quantitative map of neuron birth and integration in the adult bee brain is lacking. This gap limits our ability to understand how neural plasticity at the cellular level supports the behavioral repertoire of a superorganism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adult neurogenesis occurs in specific, behaviorally relevant regions of the honey bee brain (e.g., mushroom bodies) and that its rate is modulated by social experience, analogous to mechanisms observed in vertebrates.
proposals.method: We will adapt the vertebrate marker BrdU (5-bromo-2'-deoxyuridine) for injection into adult honey bees, followed by timed sampling and immunohistochemical detection to label proliferating cells and newborn neurons. To quantify and localize neurogenesis at scale, we will integrate this with a computational pipeline using convolutional neural networks (CNNs, specifically a U-Net architecture) for automated segmentation and counting of BrdU+ and double-labeled (BrdU+/Elav+) cells in whole-brain confocal image stacks. This will be correlated with behavioral data from automated monitoring systems (e.g., barcode tracking) to link neurogenic events to specific social interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative atlas of adult neurogenesis in the honey bee brain, directly testing the evolutionary conservation of this form of neural plasticity. It will establish a novel methodological bridge between vertebrate neuroscience and insect neuroethology, creating a framework to directly test how the birth of new neurons influences individual behavior and colony-level social network dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While BrdU is standard in vertebrates, its systematic application to map adult neurogenesis in bees in conjunction with AI-based image analysis is novel. The explicit search for conserved neurogenic mechanisms between vertebrates and a social insect, driven by precise behavioral phenotyping, represents a significant conceptual and technical cross-disciplinary leap.

proposals.title: Real-time neurochemical dynamics of the honey bee mushroom body during foraging: an in vivo microdialysis study
proposals.motivation: Honey bee foraging is a complex, socially modulated behavior critical for colony health, but its underlying neurochemical dynamics are poorly understood. Pre-2026 work has identified key genes and brain regions like the mushroom body, yet we lack direct, temporally resolved measurements of neurotransmitter flux (e.g., dopamine, octopamine, serotonin) during naturalistic behavioral states. This gap limits our ability to link molecular genetics to real-time neural circuit function in a key model for social behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct phases of the foraging cycle (departure, navigation, reward acquisition, return) are characterized by specific, rapid neurochemical signatures in the mushroom body calyces, with dopamine signaling reward expectation and octopamine modulating arousal and flight.
proposals.method: We will adapt vertebrate microdialysis for the bee brain, implanting a custom-made, miniaturized probe (1-2 mm membrane) stereotactically into the mushroom body calyx of forager bees. Bees will be harnessed or freely moving in a controlled flight arena. Dialysate will be collected at 5-minute intervals synchronized with behavioral tracking (using AI-based pose estimation from prior lab work) and analyzed via high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD). We will correlate neurochemical concentrations with behavioral transitions identified by our tracking algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct, in vivo measurements of neurotransmitter dynamics in an insect brain during a natural behavior, establishing a foundational dataset linking neuromodulation to foraging. It will validate and refine existing neurogenetic models of honey bee behavior and create a novel methodological platform for future neuroethological studies in invertebrates.
proposals.novelty: While microdialysis is standard in vertebrates, its application to the honey bee brain for real-time neurochemical sampling during behavior is unprecedented. The integration of this physiological technique with high-resolution AI-based behavioral tracking from the researcher's own recent work represents a novel, cross-methodological synthesis.

proposals.title: A cDNA Microarray Analysis of Foraging-Induced Gene Expression in the Honey Bee Mushroom Body
proposals.motivation: The honey bee mushroom body is a key neural center for learning, memory, and complex social behavior, yet the molecular-genetic pathways activated by specific natural behaviors like foraging remain poorly characterized. Pre-2026, while candidate gene studies have identified individual factors, a systems-level view of the foraging-induced transcriptome is lacking. This work will provide a foundational molecular map linking behavioral state to neural gene networks in a critical social insect model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition to foraging behavior induces a specific and reproducible gene expression signature in the mushroom bodies, encompassing genes involved in neural plasticity, metabolism, and neurotransmission.
proposals.method: We will use early cDNA microarray technology, specifically a custom array based on the Apis mellifera EST collection, to profile gene expression. Forager bees and age-matched hive bees will be collected, their mushroom bodies dissected, and total RNA extracted. Fluorescently labeled cDNA probes (Cy3/Cy5) will be synthesized and hybridized to the arrays. Expression data will be analyzed using hierarchical clustering and significance analysis of microarrays (SAM) algorithms to identify differentially expressed genes, with validation via quantitative RT-PCR.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first genome-scale profile of foraging-induced gene expression in a defined insect brain region. It will identify novel candidate genes for neural and behavioral plasticity, providing a crucial resource for subsequent functional genetic studies in honey bees and comparative analyses with other species.
proposals.novelty: While microarrays have been used in bees, applying this technology to a behaviorally defined neural tissue (mushroom bodies) in a naturalistic context is novel. It moves beyond single-gene studies to capture the coordinated molecular response underlying a complex behavioral transition.

proposals.title: From Neurons to Nectar: Modeling the Impact of Individual Brain Plasticity on Colony-Level Foraging Intelligence
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Robinson and others has established that individual honey bee brains exhibit remarkable plasticity in gene expression and neural structure in response to social and environmental cues, directly influencing behavior. However, the colony-level cognitive consequences of this distributed neural plasticity remain unknown. Understanding how population-level variation in brain states scales to collective problem-solving, like foraging efficiency, is a critical gap in social insect neuroscience and systems biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that heterogeneity in individual brain plasticity states within a colony, driven by genetic and experiential variation, creates a cognitive reservoir that enhances the colony's collective foraging efficiency and resilience to environmental change, thereby directly impacting colony fitness.
proposals.method: We will integrate existing genomic and transcriptomic data on honey bee brain plasticity with high-resolution, automated behavioral tracking (using barcode/AI methods from prior work) to parameterize an agent-based model. Individual forager agents will be endowed with internal states (e.g., response thresholds, learning rates) derived from empirical gene expression profiles. The model will simulate colony foraging in dynamic resource landscapes, using reinforcement learning algorithms (e.g., Q-learning) at the agent level to model individual learning. Colony-level performance metrics (e.g., nectar intake rate, efficiency) will be evaluated under different distributions of neural plasticity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, multi-scale model linking molecular neurobiology in individuals to colony-level cognitive performance. It will generate testable predictions about how genetic diversity and social experience shape a colony's collective intelligence. The framework will also offer a novel approach for the Earth BioGenome Project, demonstrating how sequencing data can be used to model emergent biological phenomena.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has tracked social networks or described individual plasticity, this project uniquely models the *functional cognitive output* of that plasticity at the colony scale. It moves beyond correlation to formalize a causal, mechanistic pathway from genes and brains in many individuals to the adaptive behavior of the superorganism.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Deconvolution of Caste: Quantifying the Relative Contributions of Ancient Gene Co-option and Novel Genetic Innovation in Eusocial Evolution
proposals.motivation: A central question in evolutionary biology is how complex social behaviors, like caste differentiation, evolve. Pre-2026 genomic studies have identified candidate genes and pathways, but they present a fragmented view, often focusing on single origins of eusociality. This limits our ability to distinguish between deeply conserved, rewired genetic 'toolkits' and lineage-specific novelties as the primary drivers of caste evolution. A systematic, comparative framework is needed to resolve this.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the molecular architecture of caste differentiation is a stratified mosaic: core regulatory functions are built upon ancient, conserved genes that have been transcriptionally rewired, while the modulation and fine-tuning of caste phenotypes rely more heavily on novel, lineage-specific genetic elements.
proposals.method: We will leverage genomes and transcriptomes from the Earth BioGenome Project and other pre-2026 resources for 20+ species spanning independent eusocial origins (honey bees, ants, termites, thrips) and their solitary relatives. Using orthology inference (OrthoFinder) and phylogenetic comparative methods, we will classify caste-associated genes into categories: 1) ancient, conserved; 2) ancient, rewired (expression novelty); 3) lineage-specific novel. We will then use CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing in a representative bee (Apis mellifera) and ant (e.g., Harpegnathos saltator) to disrupt genes from each category, quantifying phenotypic effects on caste morphology and behavior via automated monitoring (inspired by pre-2026 AI tracking methods).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, phylogenetically broad assessment of the evolutionary strata composing eusocial caste pathways. It will move the field from cataloging genes to understanding the fundamental principles of social trait evolution. The results will clarify whether there is a predictable 'rule' for how biological complexity builds on existing genetic material.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, quantitative deconstruction of caste pathways into evolutionary layers across multiple independent origins, followed by functional testing. This moves beyond single-species 'novelty' assays or correlative comparative studies to a causal, mechanistic framework.

proposals.title: Causal Reprogramming of Honey Bee Behavioral Ontogeny via Targeted Manipulation of Insulin/Insulin-like Signaling
proposals.motivation: Honey bee division of labor is a premier model for understanding how social behavior evolves from conserved molecular pathways. Pre-2026 correlative studies link nutrient-sensing pathways like IIS/TOR to behavioral maturation, but definitive causal evidence is lacking. Establishing direct causality is critical for understanding the mechanistic basis of behavioral plasticity and how deep evolutionary pathways are co-opted for social life.
proposals.hypothesis: Temporally precise, bidirectional manipulation of insulin/insulin-like signaling (IIS) in the fat body or brain of individual worker honey bees is sufficient to drive predictable, accelerated, or delayed transitions from nursing to foraging behavior.
proposals.method: We will use the GAL4/UAS system in transgenic bees to express thermosensitive dTRPA1 channels or inhibitory peptides (e.g., UAS-dPTEN) under control of tissue-specific promoters for IIS components. Individual, barcode-tagged bees will be housed in observation hives equipped with high-resolution cameras. Automated tracking and pose estimation AI (building on established bee pose models) will quantify nursing, walking, and foraging-related motor sequences. A recurrent neural network (RNN) classifier will analyze these sequences to assign behavioral states and detect transition timing before and after precise thermal or dietary genetic manipulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct causal evidence linking a conserved nutrient-sensing pathway to the timing of a complex behavioral state transition in a social insect. It will establish a new paradigm combining precise genetic perturbation with automated, naturalistic phenotyping to move beyond correlation in behavioral genomics. The findings will inform broader principles of how metabolic state gates behavioral development.
proposals.novelty: While IIS is correlated with bee behavior, its sufficiency to reprogram behavioral ontogeny has not been tested with temporally precise genetic tools in freely behaving individuals within a colony. The integration of conditional genetic manipulation with automated, AI-driven behavioral classification in a naturalistic social context is a novel, powerful approach to establish causality.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Systems-Level Architecture of Behavioral Plasticity: A Metabolic Control Analysis of Gene Pathways in Honey Bee Division of Labor
proposals.motivation: Honey bee behavioral maturation, a robust model of phenotypic plasticity, is regulated by conserved nutrient-sensing pathways like IIS and TOR. However, the field lacks a quantitative framework to determine the relative influence of individual pathway components on behavioral output, hindering a systems-level understanding of how genes orchestrate adaptive behavior. This gap mirrors the pre-MCA era of biochemistry, where enzyme contributions to metabolic flux were unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genes within the IIS/TOR pathways exhibit differential 'behavioral control coefficients,' analogous to enzymatic control coefficients in metabolism, with a small subset acting as high-control master regulators over the nurse-to-forager transition while others serve as permissive, modular factors.
proposals.method: We will use automated tracking (BEEtag) to quantify behavioral state transitions in cohorts of individually marked bees following precise, combinatorial perturbations of IIS/TOR pathway genes via RNAi and pharmacological agents (e.g., rapamycin). We will adapt the core MCA equations to model the behavioral transition rate as the system 'flux' and fit the model using a Bayesian inference framework to calculate control coefficients and their confidence intervals for each perturbed node.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative map of pathway control over a complex social behavior, moving beyond qualitative descriptions of gene function. It will establish a novel analytical framework, 'Behavioral Control Analysis,' applicable to other models of plasticity, and directly inform the Earth BioGenome Project by providing a method to assign functional systems-level metrics to sequenced genes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and mathematical translation of Metabolic Control Analysis from biochemistry to behavioral neuroscience, a cross-disciplinary synthesis not previously attempted, to solve the specific problem of quantifying hierarchical gene control in a behavioral system.

proposals.title: Mechanistic mapping of IIS/TOR and JH pathways onto social network plasticity in honey bee workers
proposals.motivation: Honey bee worker behavioral plasticity, exemplified by the nurse-to-forager transition, is a premier model for understanding how social context modulates brain and behavior. Pre-2026 work has implicated conserved nutrient-sensing (IIS/TOR) and reproduction-related (juvenile hormone, JH) pathways in this plasticity, but a mechanistic, gene-to-circuit understanding is lacking. This gap limits our ability to explain how fundamental life-history pathways are co-opted to regulate complex social behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic, task-specific patterns of gene expression in the IIS/TOR and JH pathways within key brain regions (e.g., mushroom bodies) directly modulate neural excitability and synaptic plasticity, thereby driving the reorganization of social interaction networks observed during behavioral maturation.
proposals.method: We will combine automated, high-resolution behavioral tracking (using barcode-based AI monitoring) of uniquely identified bees in observation hives with simultaneous, brain-region-specific transcriptomic profiling via laser-capture microdissection and RNA-seq. Bees will be sampled at defined behavioral states (nurse, forager) and during forced reversion. We will use weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to link expression modules of IIS/TOR and JH pathway genes to quantitative social network metrics (e.g., centrality, clustering coefficient) generated by the tracking system. Candidate genes will be functionally validated using RNAi knockdown followed by re-assessment of both behavior and network position.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal map linking specific, evolutionarily conserved gene pathways to the real-time dynamics of a social network. It will move beyond correlative studies to establish mechanism, revealing how fundamental physiological pathways act as 'social switches' in the brain. The findings will offer a foundational framework for understanding the genomic architecture of behavioral plasticity across social species.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, mechanistic integration of brain-specific molecular profiling with quantitative, individual-level social network analysis, moving from associative links to causal explanations of how specific gene pathways govern an individual's position and plasticity within a social collective.

proposals.title: Mapping the Social Genome: A Network-Epidemiological Framework to Link Genetic Variation to Social Role in Honey Bee Colonies
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in sociogenomics is moving beyond correlating genes with individual behavior to understanding how genetic variation shapes and is shaped by dynamic social interactions that drive colony-level outcomes. Pre-2026 advances in automated bee tracking (e.g., barcode/AI systems) and the genomic resources from initiatives like the Earth BioGenome Project provide the necessary raw data, but lack an integrative analytical framework. This gap prevents us from deciphering the rules of gene-social network interplay that underpin collective intelligence and resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic variants associated with neural function and communication will show non-random distribution across the social interaction network of a honey bee colony, and that an individual's network position (e.g., centrality, modularity) can be predicted by a combination of specific genetic markers and, in turn, predicts colony-level fitness metrics.
proposals.method: We will construct temporal social networks from high-resolution barcode tracking data of hive bees and foragers, creating a 'contact-tracing' layer. Each uniquely identified bee will be genotyped via low-coverage whole-genome sequencing, focusing on variants in candidate genes related to circadian rhythms, learning, and pheromone response. We will adapt multilayer network models and inference algorithms from epidemiological source estimation (e.g., NetRate, phylodynamic models) to treat genetic variants as 'traits' spreading through or assorting within the social network. Statistical models (e.g., network regression with permutation testing) will quantify associations between nodal genomic profiles and network metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first integrated map of genomic and social network architecture in a superorganism, directly testing sociogenomic theory. It will deliver a novel analytical pipeline for network-embedded genomic-phenotypic mapping applicable to other social species. The findings will clarify how natural selection acts on colonies through the conduit of individual social connectivity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual and methodological translation of tools from digital epidemiology and pathogen genomics—designed to infer transmission trees from genetic and contact data—to the fundamentally different problem of mapping standing genetic variation onto social interaction networks, a synthesis not previously attempted in social insect research.

proposals.title: Linking Genomic Variation to Social Network Position: A Multi-Omic Analysis of Individual Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) in a Colony Context
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that complex social behavior in honey bees is influenced by genetics and neural function, and that automated tracking can map detailed social interaction networks. However, a critical gap exists in directly linking individual genomic profiles—including variation in neurobiological and communication-related genes—to an individual's dynamic position and role within the colony's social network. This integration is essential for a systems-level understanding of how genetic variation shapes collective behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific genetic variants and brain gene expression profiles, particularly in genes related to neurotransmission, olfaction, and division of labor, will predict an individual bee's centrality and connectivity in colony-wide affiliative interaction networks.
proposals.method: We will combine established methods from the researcher's recent work: 1) Using barcode-based automated monitoring and AI (e.g., convolutional neural networks for pose estimation and interaction detection) to construct longitudinal, high-resolution social networks for multiple colonies. 2) Simultaneously, we will collect individual bees spanning network positions for whole-genome sequencing (leveraging Earth BioGenome Project pipelines) and brain transcriptomic profiling via RNA-seq. 3) We will employ network analysis metrics (e.g., eigenvector centrality, strength) and integrate them with genomic data using multivariate mixed models and gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) to identify genetic correlates of social connectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first integrated map of genomic-to-social phenotypic variation in a superorganism, directly testing how genetic diversity within a colony structures its social architecture. It will identify candidate genes and gene networks underlying social connectivity, providing a mechanistic framework for understanding behavioral resilience and division of labor. The methodology will serve as a blueprint for sociogenomic studies in other species.
proposals.novelty: While social networks and bee genomics are established fields, their integration at the individual level within functioning colonies is novel. The proposal's non-obvious strength is its real-time, longitudinal pairing of high-resolution behavioral networking with individual multi-omic profiling, moving beyond population-level genetic associations to a dynamic, individual-based sociogenomic model.

proposals.title: Mapping the Socio-Spatial Transcriptome: Single-Cell Profiling of Honey Bee Division of Labor with Automated Behavioral and Nest Architecture Registration
proposals.motivation: Division of labor in honey bees is a classic model for understanding how complex social behavior emerges from individual physiology. While prior work has linked broad behavioral states to brain gene expression, we lack a dynamic, high-resolution map of how an individual's molecular state is shaped by its precise social interactions and physical location within the colony. This gap limits our ability to understand the real-time genomic underpinnings of behavioral plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual honey bee molecular states, profiled at single-cell resolution, will form a continuous landscape defined more accurately by recent, quantifiable social experiences and spatial position in the nest than by static behavioral caste designations.
proposals.method: We will combine automated hive monitoring (using barcode-based tracking and computer vision to record individual movement, trophallaxis, and brood care) with single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) of brain and fat body tissues. Individuals will be sampled at key behavioral timepoints, with each sample registered to its complete recent behavioral history and 3D nest location. Computational integration will use dimensionality reduction (UMAP) and graph-based clustering, followed by trajectory inference (e.g., Monocle3) to model transcriptomic states as a function of the spatio-behavioral metadata.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first 'socio-spatial transcriptomic atlas' of a superorganism, directly linking high-dimensional molecular profiles to quantifiable social context. It will move the field beyond static caste comparisons, offering a new framework to dissect how social environments regulate individual genomics to sustain colony function.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated registration of single-cell molecular data with high-resolution, automated behavioral tracking and spatial mapping, an approach adapted from spatially resolved transcriptomics in developmental biology but not yet applied to a freely behaving social animal in its naturalistic context.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas9 functional validation of candidate genes underlying age-related division of labor in honey bee (Apis mellifera) brain
proposals.motivation: The molecular-genetic basis of honey bee social behavior, particularly the age-related transition from nursing to foraging, is a premier model for understanding how genes influence complex behavioral phenotypes. Pre-2026 transcriptomic and correlative studies have identified numerous candidate genes associated with this behavioral plasticity, but direct causal testing of their function in the intact organism has been limited. Establishing such causality is a critical next step for behavioral genomics and for understanding the evolution of sociality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted knockout of specific, high-confidence candidate genes (e.g., foraging, malvolio, or octopamine receptor genes) previously linked to division of labor will disrupt the normal timing or performance of nurse and foraging behaviors in adult worker honey bees.
proposals.method: We will use microinjection of CRISPR-Cas9 ribonucleoprotein complexes into early-stage honey bee embryos to generate germline or somatic mosaic knockouts of selected candidate genes. Gene-edited adult bees will be individually tagged with barcodes and introduced into observation hives. Their social networks and task performance (nursing vs. foraging) will be tracked longitudinally using the pre-existing automated monitoring platform (Robinson lab, 2022), which employs computer vision and AI to decode barcodes and classify affiliative behaviors. Behavioral profiles of edited bees will be compared to wild-type controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct functional genetic evidence for the role of specific genes in honey bee division of labor, moving beyond correlation to causation. It will establish a robust CRISPR workflow for Apis mellifera, a transformative tool for the research community. The findings will directly inform models of how genetic pathways are integrated within social contexts to produce adaptive behavior.
proposals.novelty: While CRISPR has been used in some insects, its application for functional behavioral genetics in honey bees is nascent. Combining precise gene editing with high-resolution, automated behavioral phenotyping on a colony-scale represents a novel, powerful synthesis of cutting-edge molecular and quantitative ethological methods to dissect a classic problem.

proposals.title: Beyond Gene Count: Social Buffering and Neural Plasticity as Alternative Detoxification Pathways in Honey Bees
proposals.motivation: The decline of honey bee populations is a critical pre-2026 concern, often linked to pesticide exposure. The prevailing genomic assumption is that a paucity of detoxification gene families (e.g., CYP450s, GSTs) inherently increases vulnerability. This view overlooks the sophisticated social and neural mechanisms that could mitigate toxin impact, a core theme in Robinson's work on behavior and the social brain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that honey bee colonies with fewer canonical detoxification genes can exhibit resilience to xenobiotics through compensatory mechanisms: (1) socially mediated behaviors that reduce exposure or enhance toxin excretion, and (2) neural plasticity in circuits regulating aversive learning and foraging decisions.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Earth BioGenome Project's Apis mellifera reference and conduct shallow whole-genome sequencing of colonies with known pesticide exposure histories to quantify detoxification gene repertoire. Concurrently, we will use automated monitoring (AI-tracked barcoded bees) to construct detailed social interaction networks and foraging profiles before and after sublethal exposure to a common neonicotinoid. We will combine this with brain transcriptomic analyses (RNA-seq) of key regions (e.g., mushroom bodies) to identify neural gene expression signatures of resilience, focusing on synaptic plasticity and stress response pathways rather than direct detoxification.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge a gene-centric view of toxicological vulnerability by formally integrating social genomics and neuroethology into ecotoxicology. It will identify specific behavioral and neural biomarkers of colony resilience, providing a more predictive framework for risk assessment and highlighting non-chemical avenues for supporting pollinator health.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing social and neural compensation as a functional alternative to enzymatic detoxification, moving beyond correlative gene counts to a mechanistic, systems-level understanding of resilience within the honey bee superorganism.

proposals.title: Functional Genomics of Novel Nectar and Pollen Utilization Genes in the Honey Bee (*Apis mellifera*)
proposals.motivation: The Earth BioGenome Project aims to sequence all eukaryotes, generating vast catalogs of novel genes of unknown function. In honey bees, a key pollinator, specialized genes for processing nectar and pollen are central to colony nutrition and health, but their specific biochemical roles remain poorly characterized. Determining these functions is critical for understanding the molecular basis of foraging, digestion, and social food sharing, which underpin colony resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the novel genes previously identified as associated with nectar and pollen utilization encode enzymes and transporters with specific, quantifiable roles in carbohydrate and protein metabolism, and that their expression patterns are linked to distinct behavioral states and brain functions in forager bees.
proposals.method: We will use RNA interference (RNAi) to knock down candidate novel genes in forager bees, followed by detailed phenotypic assays of nectar (sugar) and pollen (protein) metabolism using HPLC and mass spectrometry. Concurrently, we will employ automated behavioral tracking (extending methods from barcode/AI social network papers) to quantify changes in foraging efficiency and trophallaxis. Brain and gut transcriptomes of knockdown bees will be sequenced to identify dysregulated metabolic pathways and neural signaling genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional annotation for a suite of honey bee-specific genes tied to diet processing, directly linking genome sequence to organismal physiology and social behavior. It will establish a causal framework for how genetic variation in these pathways could impact colony-level foraging networks and nutritional resilience.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identified gene expression correlates, this proposal integrates targeted functional genomics (RNAi) with high-resolution automated behavioral phenotyping and metabolomics to move beyond correlation to mechanistic causation in a key social insect.

proposals.title: Comparative Genomic and Epigenomic Analysis of Solitary Hymenoptera Reveals the Molecular Basis of Sociality-Associated Phenotypes
proposals.motivation: The molecular basis of complex social behavior is a central question in evolutionary genomics, with the honey bee serving as a premier model. However, isolating genetic and epigenetic factors specific to sociality requires comparison with closely related solitary species. Pre-2026, initiatives like the Earth BioGenome Project have enabled the sequencing of diverse eukaryotes, creating an unprecedented opportunity to perform phylogenetically controlled comparisons to identify the genomic signatures of social evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solitary hymenopteran species with high odorant receptor (OR) gene family expansions or vertebrate-like DNA methylation machinery possess latent molecular substrates for social traits, and that comparative analysis will reveal core gene regulatory networks co-opted during the evolution of eusociality in the honey bee lineage.
proposals.method: We will select target solitary species (e.g., from families like Crabronidae or Sphecidae) based on pre-2026 genomic data indicating high OR counts or functional DNA methyltransferases. Using PacBio long-read and Hi-C sequencing, we will generate chromosome-level assemblies. We will annotate gene families (especially ORs and neurodevelopmental genes) using BRAKER2 and OrthoFinder, and profile brain methylomes via whole-genome bisulfite sequencing. Comparative analyses will employ phylogenetic generalized least squares models to correlate genomic/epigenomic features with behavioral traits, and machine learning (Random Forest classifiers) will identify predictive feature sets distinguishing solitary and social clades.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-quality genomic resources for key solitary hymenopteran outgroups, directly testing long-standing theories on the role of chemosensation and epigenetics in social evolution. It will identify candidate genes and regulatory pathways that are necessary pre-adaptations for sociality, moving beyond correlation to causal inference in social insect genomics.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel by using a reverse-ecological strategy: starting with molecular traits (high OR count, methylation) known in social bees and seeking them in solitary species to test their necessity and sufficiency for social evolution, rather than solely cataloging differences between established social and solitary models.

proposals.title: Decoupling Cause and Correlation: A Multi-Omic Test of Metabolic Primacy in Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) Social Behavior
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including from the Robinson lab, has established robust correlations between specific brain gene expression patterns and honey bee social behaviors like foraging. However, correlation does not imply causation. A major unresolved question is whether behavior directly drives gene expression changes, or if both are downstream outputs of a shared, underlying physiological state, such as metabolic flux. Resolving this is critical for a mechanistic understanding of behavioral plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bee's internal metabolic state, quantified by circulating metabolites and mitochondrial function, is a primary driver that coordinately regulates both brain gene expression and the performance of specific social behaviors, thereby serving as the hidden third variable explaining their correlation.
proposals.method: We will use automated hive monitoring (extending pre-2026 barcode/AI tracking) to record individual bee behavior. Simultaneously, we will perform high-frequency longitudinal sampling of the same individuals for: 1) whole-brain RNA-seq to quantify gene expression, and 2) hemolymph metabolomics via LC-MS. We will apply multivariate Granger causality analysis and latent variable modeling (e.g., Structural Equation Modeling) to the resulting time-series data to test for the precedence and predictive power of metabolic shifts relative to behavioral and transcriptomic changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond descriptive correlative models by providing a causal framework linking physiology, genomics, and behavior. It will generate a unique, publicly available multi-omic dataset for the honey bee, a key model for social behavior. The analytical approach will offer a novel template for testing driver variables in other behavioral systems.
proposals.novelty: While metabolism is known to influence behavior, directly testing its primacy over gene expression as a concurrent driver of observed behavioral states using high-resolution longitudinal multi-omics in freely behaving individuals is a novel and non-obvious integration of concepts from systems biology and behavioral neuroscience.

proposals.title: Causal Gene Network Inference for the Onset of Honey Bee Foraging via Single-Bee Longitudinal Transcriptomics and Causal Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Honey bee foraging is a classic model for behavioral maturation, but pre-2026 studies primarily identify correlative gene expression differences between pre-foraging nurses and foragers. This correlation/causation gap limits understanding of how specific genetic programs drive the behavioral transition. Resolving this is critical for integrative studies of social behavior, neural plasticity, and gene-environment interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: A core set of gene expression changes in specific brain regions, particularly involving neuropeptide and neurotransmitter pathway genes, directly causes the initiation of foraging behavior, while a larger set of observed expression differences are secondary consequences of the behavioral state change.
proposals.method: We will use longitudinal tracking of individually barcoded bees (as in Robinson's prior work) combined with repeated, non-lethal brain biopsies for single-bee RNA-seq at key pre-transition time points. Causal inference will be performed using the PCMCI (Peter-Clark Momentary Conditional Independence) algorithm, a time-series causal discovery method, to distinguish drivers from consequences within the high-dimensional transcriptomic data. Validation will involve CRISPR-Cas9 knockdowns in candidate causal genes followed by automated AI monitoring of social and foraging behavior.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first causal model of gene expression underlying a natural behavioral transition in a social insect. It will move the field beyond correlation to mechanistic understanding, identifying key drivers as potential targets for understanding behavioral plasticity. The integrated method establishes a framework for causal analysis in behavioral genomics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the longitudinal, within-individual sampling design paired with causal machine learning, moving beyond static group comparisons. This directly tests causality in gene-behavior relationships in a naturalistic context, a significant advance over pre-2026 associative studies.

proposals.title: Temporal Resolution of the Brain Transcriptomic Trajectory Underlying the Nurse-to-Forager Transition in Honey Bees (Apis mellifera)
proposals.motivation: Honey bee division of labor is a premier model for understanding the molecular basis of social behavior. While previous work has identified broad gene expression differences between nurse and forager bees, the precise sequence and dynamics of transcriptional changes during this behavioral maturation remain poorly resolved. A high-resolution temporal map is critical to distinguish initiating regulatory events from secondary consequences, advancing our understanding of how genes orchestrate behavioral plasticity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transition from nursing to foraging is governed by a tightly coordinated, sequential cascade of gene expression changes in key brain regions, with specific neuromodulatory and synaptic gene networks shifting prior to overt changes in sensory and metabolic pathways.
proposals.method: We will perform high-depth RNA-seq on mushroom bodies and antennal lobes of individually tagged bees at 12-hour intervals across the critical transition window (days 5-20 post-eclosion), leveraging automated tracking (e.g., BEEtag) to precisely determine behavioral state. Temporal expression patterns will be analyzed using weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA) and clustered with a Gaussian Process regression model to infer the sequence of regulatory events. Candidate upstream regulators will be validated via RNAi knockdown followed by automated behavioral monitoring.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first fine-scale transcriptomic timeline of a natural behavioral transition, identifying key driver genes and their temporal hierarchy. It will directly link temporal gene expression dynamics to the real-time behavioral trajectories of individually identified bees, providing a foundational dataset for models of behavioral neurogenomics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unprecedented temporal granularity of sampling paired with individual-level behavioral tracking, moving beyond static caste comparisons to model the dynamic process of behavioral maturation as a continuous trajectory.

proposals.title: A Generalized Framework for Predicting Brain Gene Expression from Social Behavior in Honey Bees (Apis mellifera)
proposals.motivation: The Robinson lab's recent work using AI to decode social networks from a single affiliative behavior (trophallaxis) demonstrates the power of computational ethology. However, a core challenge in behavioral genomics remains linking complex natural behavioral repertoires to specific brain molecular states. Pre-2026, predictive models linking behavior to gene expression are nascent and largely untested across diverse, naturally occurring behavioral categories within a single social environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning framework trained on high-resolution behavioral tracking and brain transcriptomic data can accurately predict task-specific neural gene expression profiles for other major hive behaviors beyond trophallaxis, such as nursing, guarding, or undertaking.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing barcode-based automated tracking system to collect high-dimensional behavioral data for bees engaged in nursing, guarding, and undertaking. Brain transcriptomes (via RNA-seq) will be obtained from individuals exhibiting these specific behavioral states. Using the previously established prediction framework as a base, we will train a multi-task neural network architecture (e.g., a transformer-based model or a hierarchical Bayesian regression model) to learn mappings from behavioral feature vectors (duration, frequency, social partner identity) to genome-wide co-expression modules. Model performance will be validated on held-out individuals and compared against null models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable methodology for predicting brain molecular states from natural behavior, moving beyond correlation to prediction. It will identify conserved and behavior-specific gene regulatory networks underlying the honey bee's behavioral portfolio, providing a systems-level view of behavioral regulation. The resulting open-source framework will be a resource for the Earth BioGenome Project community, enabling similar investigations in other species.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates behavior and gene expression, this proposal actively tests a *predictive* model across multiple natural behavioral splits, a significant advance from descriptive studies. The integration of automated behavioral phenotyping at hive-scale with genome-wide expression prediction for multiple tasks is a novel synthesis of pre-2026 technologies in genomics and computational ethology.

proposals.title: Mapping Social and Sensory Cues to Brain Gene Expression Reprogramming in Honey Bees (Apis mellifera)
proposals.motivation: Honey bee social behavior is orchestrated by complex, experience-dependent brain gene expression changes. While the molecular correlates of behavioral states like foraging are well-documented, the precise environmental triggers that initiate this neural reprogramming remain largely unknown. Identifying these cues is a critical gap in understanding how social information is transduced into molecular changes in the brain, a fundamental question in behavioral genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable social interaction patterns (e.g., antennal contact frequency, trophallaxis duration) and physical sensory cues (e.g., specific olfactory blends) are necessary and sufficient to trigger the early gene expression cascades associated with behavioral maturation in honey bee workers.
proposals.method: We will combine automated, high-resolution behavioral tracking (using barcode-based AI monitoring as in Crall et al., 2018) with targeted brain transcriptomics. Colonies of individually tagged bees will be exposed to controlled sensory cues (e.g., queen mandibular pheromone, brood pheromone, novel floral odors) or will have specific social interactions (nurse-forager contact) manipulated. A convolutional neural network will analyze video feeds to quantify interaction networks and individual behavior. Brain samples from cue-exposed and control bees will be analyzed via RNA-seq and qPCR for known maturation-related genes (e.g., *for*, *Egr-1*), correlating precise cue exposure histories with expression states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal map linking defined environmental stimuli to the onset of brain gene expression reprogramming, moving beyond correlation. It will provide a mechanistic model for how social signals are 'read' at the genomic level in a key model system. The integrated methodology will set a new standard for linking fine-grained behavior to molecular neuroscience in a naturalistic context.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has described gene expression differences between behavioral states, this proposal uniquely uses controlled cue delivery within a natural colony context, coupled with AI-driven behavioral quantification, to pinpoint the *initiating* triggers of the molecular cascade, which is a distinct and more causal question.

proposals.title: Targeted Gene Network Perturbation to Predict and Engineer Honey Bee Social Interaction Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Honey bee social behavior emerges from complex gene-brain-environment interactions, but predictive models linking specific gene expression to network-level behavioral outcomes are lacking. The Earth BioGenome Project's push to sequence all eukaryotes, combined with new tools for automated behavioral tracking, creates an unprecedented opportunity to move from correlation to causation in social genomics. This work directly addresses the inverse problem in behavioral genetics: can we manipulate gene expression to produce a predictable, quantifiable shift in a complex social phenotype?
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted knockdown of key genes within co-expression modules previously linked to honey bee social responsiveness (e.g., foraging, *for*) will produce predictable, quantifiable alterations in the structure of their affiliative interaction networks, as measured by automated monitoring.
proposals.method: We will use RNAi to knock down candidate genes (prioritized from existing brain transcriptomic datasets of bees exhibiting distinct social roles) in cohorts of individually barcoded honey bees. Their social interactions will be tracked continuously in observation hives using the established BEEtag automated monitoring system. We will quantify changes in individual interaction profiles and global network metrics (e.g., centrality, clustering coefficient) using the system's existing computer vision and network analysis pipeline. The observed network alterations will be compared to predictions generated from pre-perturbation gene co-expression network models.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a causal, predictive framework for moving from molecular manipulation to organismal and social network phenotypes in a key model system. It will validate the functional significance of specific gene modules for social behavior and pioneer a method for 'testing the inverse' in behavioral genomics. The results will provide a foundational protocol for probing gene-to-behavior links that can be scaled with ongoing genome sequencing efforts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates gene expression with behavior, this proposal uniquely frames the question as an inverse engineering problem, using targeted gene perturbation and high-resolution social network analysis to test *predictive* models of behavioral change. It integrates pre-2026 genomic tools, automated behavioral phenotyping, and network theory in a novel, causally oriented experimental loop.

proposals.title: Ecological Network Analysis of Brain Gene Co-Expression Societies in Honey Bee Behavioral States
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in genomics and neuroscience has established that complex behaviors, like those in honey bees, are orchestrated by dynamic ensembles of co-expressed genes. However, current network analyses (e.g., WGCNA) treat these ensembles as static graphs, missing the higher-order organizational principles that might govern their collective 'behavior'. Community ecology provides a mature theoretical framework for quantifying the structure, stability, and interactions of biological societies, which remains untapped for analyzing intra-organismal gene networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gene co-expression networks in the honey bee brain, constructed across different social and behavioral contexts, can be rigorously described as ecological communities, and that metrics of community structure (e.g., diversity, stability, nestedness) will predictably shift with behavioral state and correlate with observed social insect behavior at the organismal level.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and new RNA-seq datasets from the brains of honey bees in defined behavioral states (e.g., forager vs. nurse, aggressive vs. calm). Co-expression networks will be built using Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis (WGCNA). We will then apply ecological community metrics using R packages like 'vegan' and 'bipartite' to treat modules as species assemblages and gene connectivity as interaction strength. Key analyses will include calculating module alpha/beta diversity, testing for nestedness and modularity, and applying stability metrics from May's stability criterion to compare networks across conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel analytical framework, 'gene community ecology,' for interpreting high-dimensional genomic data. It will provide new, systems-level descriptors for brain states that link molecular network architecture to organismal behavior. The project will also generate testable predictions about how the 'society of genes' within an individual mirrors and potentially facilitates the social behavior of the colony.
proposals.novelty: While ecological network theory is applied to macro-organisms and microbiomes, its application to intra-organismal gene networks is novel. This approach moves beyond describing connectivity to quantifying the emergent properties of gene ensembles as cohesive ecological units, directly bridging concepts from Dr. Robinson's work on bee societies and genomics.

proposals.title: Quantitative Proteomic Profiling of the Honey Bee Fat Body Reveals Caste-Specific Regulatory Networks Beyond Vitellogenin
proposals.motivation: The honey bee fat body is a central metabolic and endocrine organ, with vitellogenin (Vg) playing a key role in caste differentiation, immunity, and longevity. However, pre-2026 studies have largely focused on Vg expression in isolation, leaving the broader proteomic landscape of caste-specific fat body function poorly characterized. A systems-level view is needed to understand the integrated molecular networks that establish and maintain physiological and behavioral differences between queens and workers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fat body proteomes of honey bee queens and workers differ quantitatively not only in Vg abundance but also in a suite of co-regulated proteins involved in nutrient sensing, hormone metabolism, and oxidative stress resistance, which collectively underpin caste-specific life histories.
proposals.method: We will perform label-free quantitative proteomics using high-resolution tandem mass spectrometry (e.g., Q Exactive HF-X) on fat body tissue dissected from age-matched queens and workers (n=20 per caste). Proteins will be identified and quantified using the MaxQuant software suite (version 2.x) against the *Apis mellifera* UniProt reference proteome. Differential expression analysis will be conducted with the Perseus platform, using a permutation-based FDR cutoff of <0.05 and a fold-change threshold of >2. Functional enrichment of significantly altered proteins will be analyzed via GO and KEGG pathway mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative map of caste-specific fat body proteomes, moving beyond single-gene studies. It will identify novel protein candidates and pathways involved in caste determination and social physiology, generating testable hypotheses for genetic and functional follow-up. The data will serve as a foundational resource for integrating genomics with organismal physiology in social insect research.
proposals.novelty: While Vg has been studied extensively, applying untargeted, quantitative proteomics to caste-specific fat body tissue is novel and will reveal the full protein network context in which Vg operates, uncovering previously unknown regulatory axes in social insect biology.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of insulin/IGF-1 signaling activity in the honey bee brain reveals neural correlates of social behavior plasticity
proposals.motivation: The insulin/IGF-1 signaling (IIS) pathway is a deeply conserved regulator of growth, metabolism, and lifespan, and in honey bees, it is implicated in caste determination and behavioral maturation. However, pre-2026 studies rely on static molecular snapshots, unable to capture the dynamic, cell-type-specific IIS activity in the behaving brain. Understanding how this key nutrient-sensing pathway modulates neural circuits in real-time is critical to linking social environment, physiology, and adaptive behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IIS pathway activity exhibits distinct, behaviorally relevant spatiotemporal patterns in specific brain regions and neuronal populations, and that these patterns differ between nurse and forager bees, correlating with their divergent social roles and metabolic states.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR-Cas9 to generate transgenic Apis mellifera lines with a fluorescent reporter (e.g., GFP) under the control of a conserved IIS-responsive promoter element (e.g., from dFOXO). We will combine this with automated behavioral tracking (extending our lab's AI/barcode methods) and in vivo two-photon microscopy to image reporter activity in awake, behaving bees. Image analysis will utilize pre-2026 deep learning architectures (3D U-Net) for cell segmentation and activity quantification, correlating fluorescence dynamics with simultaneously recorded social interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dynamic maps of IIS activity in an insect brain, directly linking pathway function to natural social behavior. It will establish a foundational transgenic tool for functional genomics in bees and provide mechanistic insight into how nutritional signaling pathways are co-opted to regulate complex social life histories.
proposals.novelty: While IIS gene expression is studied in bees, no pre-2026 work visualizes its real-time activity in vivo. Combining a novel transgenic reporter with established automated behavioral phenotyping creates a closed-loop system to directly observe signaling dynamics in a behaving social insect.

proposals.title: A Whole-Organism Network Model of Social Physiology in the Honey Bee: Integrating Vitellogenin, Juvenile Hormone, Insulin Signaling, Metabolism, and Reproduction
proposals.motivation: The physiological networks governing honey bee social behavior, division of labor, and aging are complex and involve key interacting systems like vitellogenin (Vg), juvenile hormone (JH), and insulin/insulin-like signaling (IIS). Pre-2026 research has detailed individual components but lacks an integrated, predictive model of how these systems interact dynamically at the whole-organism level. Such a model is critical for understanding the mechanistic basis of phenotypic plasticity and how social signals are transduced into physiological states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interactions between Vg, JH, IIS, core metabolism, and reproductive physiology form a robust, self-organizing network whose dynamic state predicts individual behavioral role and resilience. Specifically, we predict that nurse and forager phenotypes represent distinct, stable attractor states within this network model.
proposals.method: We will construct a quantitative, dynamic network model using ordinary differential equations parameterized with existing and new longitudinal data on hormone titers, gene expression (via RNA-seq), and metabolic profiles from individually tagged bees. The model will integrate known regulatory relationships (e.g., Vg-JH mutual inhibition, IIS modulation) and be trained and validated using data from bees undergoing natural and experimentally induced (e.g., social manipulation, nutritional stress) behavioral transitions. Network stability and state transitions will be analyzed using dynamical systems theory and simulated perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first predictive, whole-organism physiological network model for a social insect, moving beyond correlative studies. It will provide a computational framework to test how genetic variation and environmental cues are integrated to produce complex social phenotypes. The model will serve as a foundational tool for predicting colony-level resilience and understanding the evolution of sociality.
proposals.novelty: While the roles of Vg, JH, and IIS are well-studied in isolation, modeling their non-linear, feedback-driven interactions as a unified system to explain behavioral ontogeny is novel. This approach leverages pre-2026 'big data' from genomics and automated behavioral tracking to build a first-principles dynamical model, a significant advance over static pathway diagrams.

proposals.title: Decoupling Genomic, Plastic, and Microbiome Contributions to Eusocial Evolution: A Comparative Analysis of Apis and Bombus
proposals.motivation: A central challenge in evolutionary genomics is distinguishing the relative contributions of genome evolution, phenotypic plasticity, and host-microbiome interactions to complex traits. Eusociality in bees is a premier model for this, but prior comparative studies often conflate these drivers. The Earth BioGenome Project's ongoing efforts to sequence all eukaryotes, including diverse bee species, now provide the necessary genomic foundation to address this challenge rigorously.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core, conserved molecular pathways for social behavior, identifiable through comparative genomics of Apis (highly eusocial) and Bombus (primitively eusocial) species, will show significant modulation by both brain gene expression plasticity and gut microbiome composition, which are themselves influenced by social context.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and newly generated high-quality genome assemblies from the Earth BioGenome Project for 10+ Apis and Bombus species. Using established comparative genomics pipelines (e.g., OrthoFinder for orthogroup identification, PAML for selection tests), we will identify conserved, socially relevant gene networks. For key species (A. mellifera, B. impatiens), we will integrate brain transcriptomic (RNA-seq) data from individuals in different behavioral states and deep 16S/ITS sequencing of gut microbiomes from the same individuals. A multivariate statistical model (e.g., Partial Least Squares Path Modeling) will partition variance in behavioral phenotype scores among genomic, transcriptomic, and microbiome predictors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for partitioning evolutionary drivers in a major evolutionary transition. It will generate a prioritized list of candidate genes where evolution acts directly on coding sequence versus those where it acts on regulatory potential for plasticity or microbiome mediation. The results will inform broader debates on the genomic basis of complex traits.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined bee genomes, brain gene expression, or microbiomes in isolation, this proposal's novelty lies in their simultaneous quantification and integration within a explicit comparative phylogenetic framework to statistically test their relative contributions.

proposals.title: Functional validation of relaxed-selection genes in Apis mellifera: linking genetic drift to neural and behavioral plasticity
proposals.motivation: Comparative genomics has identified genes under relaxed selection in highly social honey bees, suggesting a potential link between reduced purifying selection and the evolution of social complexity. However, the functional roles of these genes in neural circuitry and social behavior remain entirely unknown. This gap limits our understanding of how genetic drift, alongside selection, may shape the molecular toolkit for advanced sociality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of genes under relaxed selection in Apis mellifera are expressed in the brain and modulate specific aspects of social behavior, such as affiliative interactions or task performance, thereby contributing to behavioral plasticity within the colony.
proposals.method: We will first identify candidate relaxed-selection genes from existing Apis genome alignments with solitary bees. Using in situ hybridization and single-cell RNA-sequencing of forager and nurse bee brains, we will map their spatial and cellular expression. For top candidates, we will employ RNA interference (RNAi) to knock down gene function and quantify behavioral consequences using automated, high-resolution monitoring (e.g., BEEtag system) to track individual movement and social network formation. Behavioral data will be analyzed using convolutional neural networks for pose estimation and graph-based algorithms to model changes in interaction networks post-knockdown.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional atlas linking relaxed selection to brain gene expression and social behavior in a eusocial insect. It will establish a causal framework for how non-adaptive evolutionary processes can influence social complexity. The findings will also contribute a novel set of genetic targets to the Earth BioGenome Project's functional annotation goals for eukaryotes.
proposals.novelty: While relaxed selection is often discussed in evolutionary theory, its direct functional validation in shaping a complex social phenotype is unprecedented. This proposal uniquely integrates population genomics, neurogenetics, and automated behavioral analytics to test a non-adaptive hypothesis for social evolution.

proposals.title: Comparative Gene Regulatory Network Analysis Reveals Core Modules of Increased Complexity Associated with Eusociality in Bees
proposals.motivation: The evolution of eusociality, a major transition in evolution, involves profound changes in behavior and brain function, but the specific gene regulatory network (GRN) architectures that underpin this increased behavioral and neural complexity remain poorly defined. Pre-2026 work has identified candidate genes and pathways, but a systematic, comparative network-level analysis across social species is lacking, hindering our understanding of how complex social phenotypes are genomically encoded.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the evolution of eusociality in bees is associated with the expansion and increased interconnectivity of specific, conserved gene regulatory networks, particularly those governing neural plasticity, chemosensation, and circadian rhythms, rather than the invention of entirely novel genes.
proposals.method: We will leverage the genomic resources of the Earth BioGenome Project, focusing on sequenced bee species spanning a gradient of sociality (e.g., *Apis mellifera*, *Bombus terrestris*, *Megachile rotundata*). Using single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) from key brain regions (e.g., mushroom bodies), we will construct cell-type-specific GRNs. Network complexity metrics (e.g., connectivity, modularity, hierarchy) will be quantified using established algorithms (e.g., WGCNA, GENIE3) and compared across species to identify network modules that show significant expansion and rewiring in highly eusocial lineages.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of GRN complexity changes associated with eusocial evolution, moving beyond gene lists to a systems-level understanding. It will identify core regulatory modules that are targets of selection for social behavior, offering mechanistic insights into the genomic basis of behavioral evolution and social brain specialization.
proposals.novelty: This proposal integrates comparative phylogenomics with single-cell transcriptomics and network theory specifically to quantify GRN complexity, a novel approach distinct from prior studies that focused on differential gene expression or candidate gene function in isolation.

proposals.title: Epigenetic Regulation of Evolutionary Rates in the Honey Bee Brain: Linking Social Behavior to Genomic Constraint
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that social behavior in honey bees is associated with specific brain gene expression patterns and epigenetic modifications like DNA methylation. Concurrently, the Earth BioGenome Project is generating genomic data to understand evolutionary rates. However, it remains unknown how epigenetic landscapes, which regulate gene expression and are sensitive to environmental and social cues, directly influence patterns of molecular evolution in behaviorally relevant genes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genes with high levels of regulatory epigenetic modifications (e.g., histone H3K27ac marks) in the bee brain, particularly those involved in social behavior, will exhibit slower evolutionary rates due to increased functional constraint, while genes in epigenetically permissive regions will evolve more rapidly.
proposals.method: We will integrate existing Apis mellifera brain epigenomic datasets (ChIP-seq for histone modifications, whole-genome bisulfite sequencing for DNA methylation) with comparative genomic analyses. Using genomes from the Earth BioGenome Project pipeline for multiple Apis species and outgroups, we will calculate dN/dS ratios and nucleotide diversity. We will employ a machine learning framework (e.g., random forest regression) to model evolutionary rate as a function of epigenetic state, gene ontology, and social network-associated expression patterns from automated behavioral monitoring data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between the epigenetic regulation of behavior and genome evolution, moving beyond correlative studies. It will identify a novel class of 'epigenetically constrained' genes critical for sociality and provide a predictive model for how behavioral plasticity shapes genomic architecture.
proposals.novelty: While epigenetics and evolutionary genomics are mature fields, their integration to explain rates of evolution in a behaviorally and ecologically defined gene set is novel. This approach uniquely leverages the honey bee as a model where social behavior, brain gene networks, and epigenomes are exceptionally well-characterized pre-2026.

proposals.title: Colony-Level Genomic Architecture: Linking Superorganism Genotype to Social Phenotype in Apis mellifera
proposals.motivation: The honey bee colony is a classic model of a superorganism, but its genetics are studied almost exclusively at the individual level. The Earth BioGenome Project's call to sequence all eukaryotes and new methods for automated behavioral tracking create a unique opportunity to define the colony as a holistic genomic unit. This addresses a fundamental gap in understanding how collective phenotypes emerge from the integrated genomes of thousands of related individuals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a colony's collective behavioral and physiological output (its social phenotype) is predicted by specific metrics of its internal genomic architecture, such as intracolony genetic diversity, allele frequency distributions of key behavioral genes, and the spatial distribution of patrilines within the nest.
proposals.method: We will generate high-coverage whole-genome sequences for all individuals in 50 colonies, using individual barcoding to track each bee. Colony-level genomic metrics will be calculated from this population data. These metrics will be correlated with continuous, colony-level social phenotypes (e.g., foraging tempo, thermoregulation precision) quantified via automated hive monitoring and AI-based video analysis (CNNs for pose estimation and behavior classification). Statistical modeling (mixed-effects models, network analysis) will identify genomic architectures predictive of colony performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rigorous, genomic definition of the superorganism, moving beyond metaphor to quantifiable genetics. It will produce the first 'colleytype' framework, linking multi-individual genomic variation to colony fitness. This directly advances the goals of large-scale biodiversity genomics by providing a blueprint for studying eusocial species.
proposals.novelty: While intracolony genetic diversity is known to affect colony health, no study has attempted to sequence every bee in multiple colonies to define its holistic genomic state or link that state directly to automated, high-resolution measures of collective behavior.

proposals.title: Decoupling Chronological and Behavioral Age: A Transcriptomic Analysis of Honey Bee Brain Plasticity Under Social Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Honey bee division of labor is a premier model for understanding how social experience and chronological age shape brain and behavior. Pre-2026 work by Robinson and others shows brain gene expression changes with behavioral state, but the relative dominance of intrinsic aging versus social role is unclear. Resolving this is critical for models of social evolution and neural plasticity, especially within large-scale genomic initiatives like the Earth BioGenome Project which aim to sequence all eukaryotes, providing a comparative framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under extreme social manipulation, behavioral role will exert a stronger influence on brain gene expression profiles than chronological age, overriding typical age-associated transcriptional patterns.
proposals.method: We will use automated monitoring with barcodes and AI (as in the referenced social networks paper) to track individual Apis mellifera bees in observation hives. We will create 'extreme conditions' by inducing precocious foragers (young bees performing old-bee behavior) and reverted nurses (old bees performing young-bee behavior) via social manipulation. Brain transcriptomes of these groups, along with age-matched and behavior-matched controls, will be sequenced. We will use a multivariate statistical framework (e.g., Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) and machine learning classifiers (e.g., Random Forest) to quantify the variance in expression explained by age versus behavior.
proposals.contribution: This will directly test the primacy of social versus chronological factors in shaping the brain's molecular state, providing a quantitative framework for understanding behavioral plasticity. It will generate a high-resolution dataset linking precise behavioral tracking (via AI) to brain genomics, serving as a model for integrative analysis within the Earth BioGenome Project's ethos.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies correlate expression with age or behavior, this experiment actively decouples them to test causality. The integration of continuous AI-based behavioral phenotyping with brain transcriptomics in a single, controlled experiment is a novel, systems-level approach.

proposals.title: Disentangling Adaptive and Stress-Induced Transcriptional Noise in the Honey Bee Brain: A Multi-Omics and AI-Enabled Approach
